raw_text: In the early 1990s Gilberths found that the key to improving work efficiency was in reducing unnecessary motions. Not only were some motions unnecessary, but they caused employee fatigue and increased opportunity for unsafe events to occur.
asset_rel_path: assets/source_files/ENP111/01 Intro to HF and Risk (1).pptx

raw_text: The work of the Gilbreths is often associated with that of Frederick Winslow Taylor, yet there was a substantial philosophical difference between the Gilbreths and Taylor. The symbol of Taylorism was the stopwatch; Taylor was concerned primarily with reducing process times. The Gilbreths, in contrast, sought to make processes more efficient by reducing the motions involved. They saw their approach as more concerned with workers' welfare than Taylor, which is often perceived as concerned mainly with profit.
asset_rel_path: assets/source_files/ENP111/01 Intro to HF and Risk (1).pptx
assets/source_files/ENP111/01 Intro to HF and Risk (1).pptx

raw_text: Towards the end of WW2, the military started to realize that no matter how much training and job selection went into task performance, the complex equipment still exceeded the capabilities of the people who had to operate it. And, so they were forced to reconsider fitting the equipment to the person.
asset_rel_path: assets/source_files/ENP111/01 Intro to HF and Risk (1).pptx

raw_text: Research in this period laid the foundation for understanding human movement and response time as well as the relationship between human performance and system design. Broadbent’s work is especially notable because it occurred at a time when Behaviorism dominated psychology departments. The idea of studying unobservable aspects of the human ‘system’ was novel and challenged the status quo. Given that fact, some have suggested that Broadbent was the first cognitive psychology.
asset_rel_path: assets/source_files/ENP111/01 Intro to HF and Risk (1).pptx

raw_text: With the advent of human space flight, human factors took off. (pun intended).
asset_rel_path: assets/source_files/ENP111/01 Intro to HF and Risk (1).pptx

raw_text: NASA was quick to realize that the more human element in this system was most difficult to predict and engineer, and therefore the most likely to fail unpredictably.
asset_rel_path: assets/source_files/ENP111/01 Intro to HF and Risk (1).pptx

raw_text: They invested heavily in human factors, developed many new models for cognitive workload assessment. Conducted both psychological and ergonomic research.

raw_text: What we usually think of as “memory” in day-to-day usage is actually long-term, but there are also important short-term and sensory memory processes, which must be worked through before a long-term memory can be established. The different types of memory each have their own particular mode of operation, but they all cooperate in the process of memorization, and are the three necessary steps in forming a lasting memory.
asset_rel_path: assets/source_files/ENP111/01 Intro to HF and Risk (1).pptx

raw_text: The sensory memory for visual stimuli is sometimes known as the iconic memory, the memory for aural stimuli is known as thee echoic memory, and that for touch as the haptic memory. Look at bright light, and you close your eyes, you still see white spots for a few seconds.
asset_rel_path: assets/source_files/ENP111/01 Intro to HF and Risk (1).pptx

raw_text: Sensory memory is an ultra-short-term memory and decays or degrades very quickly, typically in the region of 200 - 500 milliseconds (1/5 - 1/2 second) after the perception of an item, and certainly less than a second (although echoic memory is now thought to last a little longer, up to perhaps three or four seconds).
asset_rel_path: assets/source_files/ENP111/01 Intro to HF and Risk (1).pptx

raw_text: Information is passed from the sensory memory into short-term memory via the process of attention (the cognitive process of selectively concentrating on one aspect of the environment while ignoring other things), which effectively filters the stimuli to only those which are of interest at any given time.
asset_rel_path: assets/source_files/ENP111/01 Intro to HF and Risk (1).pptx

raw_text: Smell may actally be even more closely linked to memory than the other senses, possibly because the olfactory bulb and olfactory cortex (where smell sensations are processed) are physically very close - separated by just 2 or 3 synapses - to the hippocampus and amygdala (which are involved in memory processes). Thus, smells may be more quickly and more strongly associated with memories and their associated emotions than the other senses, and memories of a smell may persist for longer –
asset_rel_path: assets/source_files/ENP111/01 Intro to HF and Risk (1).pptx

raw_text: Short-term memory acts as a kind of “scratch-pad” for temporary recall of the information which is being processed at any point in time, and has been refered to as "the brain's Post-it note". It can be thought of as the ability to remember and process information at the same time. It holds a small amount of information (typically around 7 items or even less) in mind in an active, readily-available state for a short period of time (typically from 10 to 15 seconds, or sometimes up to a minute).
asset_rel_path: assets/source_files/ENP111/01 Intro to HF and Risk (1).pptx

raw_text: Long-term memory is, obviously enough, intended for storage of information over a long period of time. Despite our everyday impressions of forgetting, it seems likely that long-term memory actually decays very little over time, and can store a almost unlimited amount of information almost indefinitely.
asset_rel_path: assets/source_files/ENP111/01 Intro to HF and Risk (1).pptx

raw_text: There is actually  some debate as to whether we actually ever “forget” anything at all, or whether it just becomes increasingly difficult to access or retrieve certain items from memory.
asset_rel_path: assets/source_files/ENP111/01 Intro to HF and Risk (1).pptx

raw_text: Basic Definition: At its core, risk refers to the possibility of an adverse event occurring and the uncertainty about its outcome.
asset_rel_path: assets/source_files/ENP111/01 Intro to HF and Risk (1).pptx
assets/source_files/ENP111/01 Intro to HF and Risk (1).pptx

raw_text: Academic: Choosing to not study for a major exam poses a risk of failing.
asset_rel_path: assets/source_files/ENP111/01 Intro to HF and Risk (1).pptx

raw_text: Financial: Spending your savings on a spring break trip might mean you won't have money for textbooks next semester.
asset_rel_path: assets/source_files/ENP111/01 Intro to HF and Risk (1).pptx

raw_text: Social: Going to a party the night before an early class can risk your attendance or attentiveness in that class.
asset_rel_path: assets/source_files/ENP111/01 Intro to HF and Risk (1).pptx
assets/source_files/ENP111/01 Intro to HF and Risk (1).pptx

raw_text: Risk can be measured and quantified. For example, if you've skipped 50% of your classes, you can statistically analyze your risk of failing based on past patterns.
asset_rel_path: assets/source_files/ENP111/01 Intro to HF and Risk (1).pptx

raw_text: Uncertainty, on the other hand, involves situations where outcomes are unpredictable. For example, even if you've attended all your classes and studied hard, there's still some uncertainty about what questions will appear on your final exam.
asset_rel_path: assets/source_files/ENP111/01 Intro to HF and Risk (1).pptx

raw_text: Risk-Reward Tradeoff: Often, greater risks come with the possibility of greater rewards. But higher potential rewards might not always be worth the risks.
asset_rel_path: assets/source_files/ENP111/01 Intro to HF and Risk (1).pptx

raw_text: Career: Pursuing a start-up right after college might offer big potential rewards but also has a high risk compared to a stable entry-level job.
asset_rel_path: assets/source_files/ENP111/01 Intro to HF and Risk (1).pptx

raw_text: Investments: Stocks might offer higher returns than bonds, but they also come with higher volatility.
asset_rel_path: assets/source_files/ENP111/01 Intro to HF and Risk (1).pptx

raw_text: Risk Management: Just because risks exist doesn't mean they should always be avoided. Instead, they should be managed.
asset_rel_path: assets/source_files/ENP111/01 Intro to HF and Risk (1).pptx

raw_text: Prioritization: Focus on studying for an exam worth 50% of your grade versus one worth 5%.
asset_rel_path: assets/source_files/ENP111/01 Intro to HF and Risk (1).pptx

raw_text: Diversification: Instead of putting all your summer earnings into one stock, consider spreading it out over multiple investments.
asset_rel_path: assets/source_files/ENP111/01 Intro to HF and Risk (1).pptx

raw_text: Insurance: Like how health insurance can help cover unexpected medical bills, there are ways to hedge or insure against certain risks.
asset_rel_path: assets/source_files/ENP111/01 Intro to HF and Risk (1).pptx

raw_text: Personal Risk Tolerance: Different people have different appetites for risk based on their personality, life experiences, and current circumstances. Some may be more willing to take on debt to pursue a graduate degree, believing in the future payoff, while others prefer a more conservative financial approach.

Cognitive Limitations: Every human has cognitive boundaries. Memory lapses, attention deficits, misjudgments, and misperceptions are inherently internal. For instance, forgetting a step in a procedure or misunderstanding instructions is rooted in our cognitive processes.
Emotional Factors: Emotions can significantly influence decision-making and actions. Stress, anxiety, overconfidence, or complacency are internal states that can lead to errors.

Physical Limitations: Fatigue, decreased reaction time, and other physiological states can cause errors. A tired pilot or a sleep-deprived surgeon is more likely to make mistakes due to their internal physical state.
Decision-making Biases: Cognitive biases like confirmation bias, overconfidence bias, and availability heuristic are internal mental shortcuts that can lead to erroneous decisions.
Are Errors truly “human”: Internal vs External

System Design Flaws: Poorly designed systems, tools, or procedures can set individuals up for failure. For instance, if a piece of equipment is designed unintuitively, users might make mistakes while operating it.

Environmentally Induced: External factors like lighting, noise, temperature, or other environmental conditions can contribute to errors. For instance, a worker in a loud environment might mishear instructions.
Training and Guidance: Lack of proper training or guidance is an external factor. If a person is not given the right information or skills to perform a task, they are more likely to err.
Organizational Culture: A culture that does not prioritize safety, rushes tasks, or does not provide adequate resources creates an environment ripe for errors.
External Pressures: Deadlines, societal expectations, peer pressure, or even customer demands can push individuals to make hurried decisions or skip safety protocols, leading to errors.
Ambiguous Information: Externally provided information that is unclear or ambiguous can lead individuals to make wrong decisions or take incorrect actions.
Are Errors truly “human”: Internal vs External
Latent Errors: Hidden mistakes embedded in systems, often introduced by designers, engineers, policy decision makers
Active Errors: These are direct mistakes, often made by frontline operators, and their effects are immediately apparent.

Does automation lead to less human error or more?
Think of arguments for each side:
Automated systems can perform repetitive tasks consistently without being affected by “human factors” including fatigue, boredom, or stress
Automation can reduce the cognitive load placed on humans in a system, thus reducing the probability of error(s) caused by cognitive load or distraction
Automated systems can operate continuously without the need for breaks or rest, reducing the likelihood of errors caused by fatigue
Automated systems can incorporate error-checking and fail-safes to prevent or detect errors, mitigating their potential impact
In high-risk domains such as nuclear power or aviation, automated systems can respond to multiple inputs with greater speed than human operators
Automated systems may struggle to adapt to unforeseen or complex situations
People may become overly reliant on automation, leading to a reduced ability to respond effectively when automation fails or requires human input
Design flaws/errors automated systems can lead to unintended/unanticipated consequences, including errors in outputs to humans in the system
Loss of Skills for People in the System
Over time, human skills related to automated tasks may degrade. If human intervention becomes necessary, people may struggle to perform effectively

A system is resilient if it can adjust its functioning prior to, during, or following events (changes, disturbances, and opportunities), and thereby sustain required operations under both expected and unexpected conditions.

Good reference here: “The Field Guide to Understanding Human Error”, Sidney Dekker

Internal Perspective: From a cognitive standpoint, errors can result from various mental processes including perception, memory, and reasoning. For instance, people might misperceive information, forget crucial details, or make incorrect inferences.

External Perspective: Rather than viewing errors as solely the fault of individuals, many experts believe that errors often arise from systemic issues. In other words, while an individual might commit an error, the root cause might be a poorly designed system that sets the stage for mistakes. The Swiss cheese model by James Reason is a famous analogy in this perspective. In this model, errors happen when the holes in different layers (slices of cheese) align, allowing a threat (often depicted as a trajectory) to pass through.
Often the end user is blamed because, they are the last and more visible layer in an “accident.”
Error associated with the performance of the front-line operators. Effects are typically immediately evident.
i.e., pilots, air traffic controllers, control rooms crews, etc
Related to activities removed in time and space form the direct control interface

i.e., designers, managers, maintenance, supervisors. Designing something incorrectly can be a blunt end. These are hidden mistakes embedded in systems, often introduced by designers or decision-makers. They may lie dormant and only become evident when combined with other factors.

James Reason, described latent errors as the "resident pathogens" within a system. While they might not cause harm by themselves, when aligned with other circumstances (akin to the holes aligning in the Swiss cheese model), they can lead to catastrophic outcomes.

The Swiss Cheese Model illustrates such errors as potential “holes” in layers of defense. Latent errors contribute to vulnerabilities in that defense such that when active errors align with them, a path for adverse events is formed. The model emphasizes the need for a resilient system with multiple layers of defense to prevent such alignments.

Origin: Latent errors often arise from decisions made by designers, management, or others not directly involved in frontline operations. They can be embedded into the system long before an actual incident occurs

Example: Inadequate maintenance protocols for a piece of machinery might be established due to a management decision. Even if everything runs smoothly at first, over time, the machinery might fail, leading to an accident.
Detectability: Because latent errors can be dormant, they often go unnoticed until they interact with active errors or specific circumstances that bring them to the surface.

Example: A hospital might have a storage system where medications with similar packaging are stored next to each other. No incidents occur until one day, a fatigued nurse mistakenly picks up the wrong medication, leading to patient harm.
Systemic Nature: Latent errors are typically not about individual negligence but are more systemic, tied to organizational culture, policies, or design.
Example: A company's culture that discourages open communication may lead to employees not reporting minor issues. Over time, these unreported issues might compound and result in a major problem.

Malicious compliance, also known as "work-to-rule," is the act of intentionally following instructions, rules, or laws in such a way that it produces an undesirable outcome for the person or entity that set those instructions. This is usually done as a form of protest, resistance, or to highlight the inadequacy or absurdity of the rules themselves. Instead of openly refusing to comply, individuals carry out the orders or rules in a literal or overzealous manner, knowing that doing so will cause an issue or problem.
Accident is a sequence of events that led to the way to an unplanned undesirable effect (failure). An incident is a similar progression with one difference - it was stopped in time.

So incidents and accidents are similar in substance, but with only a different outcome. Very often, after some accident, investigators will find that there were several incidents of the same exact nature as the accident but had been recovered from before the negative consequences.

It's worth noting that the distinction between these two terms can sometimes be subtle, and usage may vary based on industry or context. For instance, in some safety and regulatory contexts, all unplanned events, including those that result in harm, might be referred to as "incidents" for consistency in reporting and analysis.
Now, is an incident the same as a near miss?

There’s a model, called the iceberg model, that postulates that number if accidents that happen are directly correlated to the number of incidents by some ratio. A typical ratio is 1 accident per 10 incidents for 30 near misses for 600 unsafe acts. However, this is a little questionable, especially given the recent trends in automation.

Automation has the potential to reduce certain types of human errors, BUT it is not a panacea. The effectiveness of automation depends on the context, the nature of the tasks involved, and consideration of potential drawbacks and risks. A balanced approach applying the strengths of automation with human oversight and intervention may be required to optimize performance and to control errors.

Resilience Engineering is an approach to safety management that shifts the focus from preventing negative outcomes to promoting the adaptive capacities that enable systems to function under varying conditions, including during unexpected events or disturbances. Inste

In the context of human error, Resilience Engineering recognizes that humans Are Inherently Fallible: No matter how well-trained or experienced, people will make mistakes. Instead of trying to eliminate human error entirely (which is unrealistic), Resilience Engineering aims to create systems that can absorb the variability of human behavior and continue to operate safely.
Respond: Systems should be able to effectively respond to threats or disturbances.
Monitor: Systems should be able to monitor their own performance and the environment in which they operate.
Learn: Systems should be able to learn from both failures and successes.
Anticipate: Systems should be prepared for future challenges by anticipating potential disturbances and risks.
Physical Barriers: Robust containment structures prevent the release of radioactive materials.
Reactor Cooling: Redundant cooling systems maintain safe core temperatures.
Control and Monitoring: Automated systems detect anomalies, triggering alarms and safety responses.
Emergency Shutdown: Rapid shutdown systems halt the reactor if significant anomalies are detected.
Isolation and Redundancy: Critical systems are isolated, and redundant safety measures prevent cascading failures.
Emergency Preparedness: Comprehensive plans and coordination with off-site response organizations ensure effective crisis management.
Regulatory Oversight: Strict safety standards, continuous improvement, and incident analyses contribute to a resilient and safe nuclear power plant design.

In healthcare settings, human error can have severe consequences. Thus, resilience in this context doesn't only mean bouncing back from external shocks but also preventing, mitigating, or recovering from errors that could harm patients.
Features that Contribute to the Resilience of Hospitals in Light of Human Error:
Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs): Hospitals have protocols for most procedures, ensuring consistency and reducing the likelihood of mistakes.

Checklists: For many procedures, especially surgeries, checklists are used to ensure every step is followed correctly and necessary preparations are made. This can catch potential errors before they impact patient care.

Double-checking and Peer Reviews: Critical tasks, like administering certain medications or interpreting lab results, often require a second opinion or a colleague's review. Use of technology, for example, Bar-coded Medication Administration: bar-code systems to ensure that the right patient is getting the right medication at the right dose and time.
Training and Simulations: Medical staff often undergo simulations for emergency scenarios, complex surgeries, or rare medical situations to prepare them for real-life scenarios.

Fail-safes and Alarms: Medical equipment often comes with alarms and fail-safes to alert caregivers to abnormalities, such as a dislodged IV, a patient's declining vital signs, or equipment malfunction.
Cross-disciplinary Rounds: Medical teams from various specialties may perform rounds together, allowing diverse expertise to weigh in on patient care, catching potential oversights.

Continual Training and Education: Medicine is always evolving, and continual education ensures that medical staff are updated on best practices, reducing the likelihood of error based on outdated knowledge.

The Perception, Cognition, and manual Action (PCA) model may be used to further break down user interactions into specific user actions related to perceptual inputs, cognitive processing, and physical actions involved in performing the related step. [3].
Using the PCA model, tasks are decomposed down to the level of individual user interactions related to:
1) Perception – Perceptual information to be noticed or detected by the user.
2) Cognition – Cognitive components to be interpreted by the user.
3) Action – Physical actions taken by the user.

Using the PCA model assists manufacturers in understanding the different PCA elements required for each task and further delineate use errors that may occur if the user if unable to meet these requirements. Additional steps or tasks may be revealed or uncovered that manufacturers were previously unaware of or may have glanced over in the initial task list.
Potential use-related problems can be identified by asking the following questions [2]:
1) What if the user is unable to perceive x?
2) What if the user is unable to interpret/process y?
3) What if the user is unable to perform action z?

Although use errors are ultimately bound to an action (or inaction), analysis of perceptual information and cognitive elements may inform the cause of use errors that may occur. This breakdown of use errors then allow manufacturers to further inform their use-related risk analysis (URRA).

Hierarchical Task Analysis (HTA): HTA is one of the most widely used methods. It breaks down complex tasks into a hierarchical structure, showing the relationships between higher-level goals and lower-level sub-tasks. HTA is often used to design and evaluate user interfaces and to identify potential usability issues.

Cognitive Task Analysis (CTA): CTA focuses on understanding the cognitive processes, strategies, and decision-making involved in completing a task. It is particularly useful in complex domains such as aviation, healthcare, and military operations. Techniques like think-aloud protocols, cognitive interviews, and knowledge elicitation are employed in CTA.

Event-Based Task Analysis: Event-based analysis focuses on specific events or incidents within a task. It is useful for understanding how users respond to unexpected situations and how system design can support effective responses

As a reminder the HF process in medical device development is important because medical devices, whether they are simple tools or complex machines, are often used in critical situations where a mistake can have serious consequences. A design that does not consider the user's needs, abilities, and limitations can lead to errors and patient harm.

The overall process in begins by understanding who the users are, their needs, and the environments in which they will use the device, then we conduct an iterative use-related risk analysis. We then design the device, making sure to incorporate human factors principles into the design process to make the UI resistant to use errors (and to also increase usability and user satisfaction). As we design the device we update our use-related risk analysis to match the device design and uncover if any new use related risks have been introduced and we conduct testing along the way to ensure that the designs that we develop are effective in controlling risk.
In other lectures conducting a task analysis to identify potential use errors, as well as how to write good use errors.

But its also important to note that there are many other sources of use errors that we need to look to conduct a comprehensive use related risk analysis. In this lecture we will work though an example of going through from a task analysis to a URRA document.

A KPA helps identify how people have made mistakes or otherwise struggled using products similar to the one being developed. This can be your own products, a previous version of it, or similar ones you have in the market, or it could be products that competitors might make. Over even a product with just a similar user interfaces, user interactions and/or uses. Online data-sources, such as Manufacturer and User Facility Device Experience (MAUDE). Are a great source of data. Manufacturer and User Facility Device Experience (MAUDE) database represents reports of adverse events involving medical devices.
The searchable database contains the last 10 years of medical device report (MDR) data.

When is it time to make your final URRA draft: once we have completed the process, our design is locked, we develop a final draft of our URRA. This final draft becomes the basis of the validation study. I say final draft because if we’ve done our jobs correctly, we shouldn’t expect to see any new use errors and our results should validate that all of our implemented are effective. But that’s not always the case, so you can’t really finalize your URRA until after you have had a successful validation study.

When developing a product, you need to identify all of the different use errors – or mistakes – a user could potentially make.

In previous sessions, you’ve learned about different methods for identifying these use errors, and why that’s important to developing a use-related risk analysis. Today we’ll cover a specific method for identifying use errors, which is Known Problems Analysis.

At a high level, Known Problems Analysis (or a KPA), is the process of reviewing use-related issues that have occurred with devices that are already on the market that are similar to the new one being developed. The idea is to understand these issues, and then design the new product so that it isn’t prone to same sorts of known problems. So basically, don’t let history repeat itself, and instead, make the new product better.

Risk Management File (RMF): Set of records and other documents that are produced by risk management
Severity: Measure of the possible consequences of a hazard

Risk analysis: Systematic use of available information to identify hazards and to estimate the risk
Risk Analysis is the process used to identify hazards and to estimate risk. It includes identifying:
The intended use, intended users, and intended use environment of the device*
Reasonably foreseeable misuses of the device*
The characteristics of the device that could impact safety
The Hazards associated with design, manufacturing, and use of the device
Hazardous situations that could arise from the hazards (i.e. how people, property, the environment can be exposed to a hazard)
The resulting harms (i.e. consequences of the exposure to the hazardous situation)
Estimating the risk (i.e., the severity of the harm the probability of the harm occurring)
* Intended use, users and use environment as well as foreseeable misuses are inputs to the risk analysis process from the “Use Specification” - an output of the Usability Engineering process

All risk analysis is based on the device’s intended use, intended users, and intended use environment and must include “reasonably foreseeable misuses” of the device and consider risks arising in in normal and fault conditions
Hazards can be associated with design (including software), manufacturing, and use of the device as well as its distribution, storage and servicing
A hazardous situation occurs when people, property or the environment are exposed to a potential source of harm (hazard) through a sequence of events. (Not all hazards lead to hazardous situations)
An injury (harm) can only occur after exposure to the hazard
A hazardous situation may lead to different harms
Clinical expertise is necessary to identify harms and estimate the consequences of the harms (severity)

Risk estimation is the process used to assign values to the probability of occurrence of harm and the severity of that harm.
Severity of harm is a continuum that will depend on numerous factors (such as the level of exposure to the hazardous situation, a patient’s pre-existing conditions and co-morbidities)
To simply risk analysis a few discrete levels are generally used to categorize severity using common terms and descriptors appropriate for the medical device
Each harm is assigned a severity level which is determined using clinical/medical expertise and can be based on the reasonable worst-case consequence
The severity rating does not include probability

Like severity, probability is also a continuum and discrete levels are generally used to categorize it

The probability of harm is sometimes broken into two parts – the probability of the hazardous situation occurring (P1) and the probability that the harm of specified severity occurs when the hazardous situation has occurred (P2)
Examples of common probability terms and descriptions from ISO 24971

P1 includes elements of the sequence of events that lead to the exposure to the hazard. The individuals assigned to estimate the probability P1 should be familiar with the design, construction, use and servicing of the medical device, as well as have an adequate understanding of the use environment.
The P2 estimate will include a clinical aspect thus the individuals assigned to estimate P2 should be familiar with the medical use of the device
P2 is often estimated using expert clinical judgment and experience with similar devices, informed by adverse event data, medical literature and information from post-production.
Detailed understanding of the performance of the medical device is generally not needed to estimate P2
Combining the severity of harm and the probability of harm produces the risk estimate
A risk matrix is often used to show the combination of the severity level and the probability of harm level (S x Ph)
Each Severity x Probability of harm combination is assigned a risk level (e.g., Low, Medium, High) that is then used to evaluate the risk
Risk evaluation: Process of comparing the estimated risk against given risk criteria to determine the acceptability of the risk
Risk evaluation is used to determine if risk control is required to reduce a risk to an acceptable level
The criteria used to determine the acceptability of individual risks is defined prior to starting risk analysis

ISO 14971 does not specify risk acceptability criteria since what is acceptable differs by culture, regulatory agencies and even at an individual level. Thus, each medical device manufacturer is required to define its own risk acceptability criteria
Residual risk is acceptable and additional risk reduction/risk control measures are not required.

Residual risk is broadly acceptable. However, risk reduction should be investigated. Application of “state of the art” risk control measures such as meeting safety requirements identified in applicable standards shall be considered to reduce risk to an acceptable level.

Residual risk is unacceptable and additional risk control measures shall be implemented. If implementing additional risk control measures is not possible a benefit risk analysis must be performed to ensure that the clinical/medical benefits outweigh the residual risk.
Device manufacturers often use the risk levels determined during risk estimation as a starting point for risk acceptability.
Low risks are often deemed acceptable without risk reduction being required
Medium and high risks will require additional action to reduce the risk
Risk must be reduced “As far as possible” considering generally accepted state of the art technology
Requirements from relevant safety standards have been implemented
Risk control: Process in which decisions are made and measures implemented by which risks are reduced to, or maintained within, specified levels.
Risk Control is a process step in ISO 14971 that includes:
Identifying and analyzing risks arising from risk control measures
All risks that do not fall into an acceptable range must be reduced
Risk Control Measures (RCM) are used to reduce risks
All risks that do not fall into an acceptable range must be reduced
If Risk Reduction is not possible, a benefit-risk analysis must be performed to ensure that the clinical benefits outweigh the risk
Risk Control Measures (RCM) are used to reduce risks
The term “risk control” is often used interchangeably for risk control measures and the ISO 14971 risk control process step
A main focus of device RM should be on reducing identified risks - not in risk estimation
ISO 14971 lists three types/categories of risk control measures:
protective measures in the medical device itself or in the manufacturing process
information for safety and, where appropriate, training to user
Priority should be given to inherent safety risk control measures followed by protective measures and finally information for safety
Multiple risk control measures may be required to reduce a risk to an acceptable level
Testing is not considered a risk control measure under ISO 14971
All RCMs must be implemented and verified to be effective in reducing risk
Eliminating the hazard of sharp edges that can cause injury by designing the surfaces with rounded edges.
Eliminating the hazard of electric shock by using a manually operated pump instead of an electrical pump.
- reducing the probability of occurrence of the harm
Reducing the probability of fibrillation harm due to an electric shock by having no accessible live parts. Reducing the probability of unauthorized access to data by identity management.
Reducing the probability of biological reactions due to microbial contamination by using cleanroom technologies or sterilization.
- reducing the severity of the harm
Reducing the severity of harm from being squeezed by a moving part by using a low-power motor and low speed.
Reducing the severity of harm from an electric shock by using low electric voltage (below 42 V).
Examples of Inherent Safety RCMs for use-related hazards:
• Use specific connectors that cannot be connected to the wrong component.
• Remove features that can be mistakenly selected or eliminate an interaction when it could lead to use error.
• Improve the detectability or readability of controls, labels, and displays.
• Automate device functions that are prone to use error when users perform the task manually.
Examples of Protective Measure RCMs for use-related hazards:
- preventing the occurrence of a hazardous situation
Using automatic cut-off or over-pressure valves.
Protective covers of electrical wires and power units (covered plugs, sockets and connectors).
Guards for moving parts or to prevent patients falling off a table or out of bed. Inspection testing in manufacturing to detect non-conforming products.
- preventing a hazardous situation from leading to harm
Using visual or acoustic alarms to alert the user to a hazardous situation.
Protective measures in the medical device itself or in the manufacturing process
• Incorporate safety mechanisms such as physical safety guards, shielded elements, or software or hardware interlocks.
• Include warning screens to advise the user of essential conditions that should exist prior to proceeding with device use, such as specific data entry.
• Use alerts for hazardous conditions, such as a “low battery” alert when an unexpected loss of the device’s operation could cause harm or death.
• Use device technologies that require less maintenance or are “maintenance free.”
Examples of Information for Safety RCMs:
- placing warnings on the medical device;
Warning: Do not use after [expiry date].
- including contra-indications in the accompanying documentation;
- providing instructions to support correct use and to avoid use error;

Apply epinephrine injector to the middle of your outer thigh (upper leg), through clothing if necessary. Do not inject into your veins, buttocks, fingers, toes, hands or feet. Hold the leg of young children firmly in place before and during injection to prevent injuries.
- providing instructions to use personal protective equipment;
Use gloves and eyeglasses when handling toxic or hazardous materials.
- providing instructions about measures to reduce the severity of harm;
Rinse immediately with water after contamination with hazardous substances.
- providing training to users on how to use the medical device correctly;
Training program for operators of radiotherapy equipment or for home-use dialysis machines. Note: Operators may be required to certify to training completion.
- providing instructions relating to installation and maintenance during the lifetime of the medical device
Maintenance intervals, maximum expected lifetime, how to dispose of the medical device properly.

Objective evidence must be provided to show that all risk control measures have been implemented and that the RCMs effectively reduce risk
Examples of RCM verification of implementation may include design documentation, design specifications, manufacturing process documentation and process qualification
Examples of verification of RCM effectiveness may include design verification, design validation – including usability validation, and manufacturing process validation

Residual risk is the risk remaining after risk control measures have been implemented and verified to be effective
The risk is re-estimated and re-evaluated using the same risk estimation and risk evaluation process described in detail above
If a residual risk is not judged acceptable using the defined acceptability criteria, further risk control measures must be considered
If further risk control is not practicable for an unacceptable risk, a benefit-risk analysis must be performed to determine if the benefits of the intended use outweigh the residual risk
If the benefits do not outweigh the residual risk, then the manufacturer may consider modifying the medical device or its intended use. Otherwise, the risk remains unacceptable

After all risk control measures have been implemented and verified, the manufacturer shall evaluate the overall residual risk posed by the medical device, taking into account the contributions of all residual risks, in relation to the benefits of the intended use, using the method and the criteria for acceptability of the overall residual risk defined in the risk management plan
Evaluation of Overall Residual Risk (vs. Benefit)
The evaluation of overall residual risk is the point where residual risk is viewed from a broad perspective

Even though all individual risks have been reduced to an acceptable level (or have been accepted based on benefit-risk analysis) from the risk control process step, the overall device risk must be evaluated against the benefit the device provides:
Benefit: positive impact or desirable outcome of the use of a medical device on the health of an individual, or a positive impact on patient management or public health

This step is particularly important for complex medical devices and for medical devices with many individual risks. The evaluation can lead to the conclusion that the medical device is safe and effective – but only if its benefits outweigh its risks

The overall residual risk must be evaluated by persons with the knowledge, experience and authority to perform such tasks. Ultimately, the evaluation should be based on expert judgment with essential roles for application knowledge and clinical expertise
Only when a device’s clinical benefit exceeds its overall residual risk can it be commercialized

Perhaps one of the more valuable results of reviewing a series of accident reports is
an appreciation for the complex chain of events which often underlies an accident.
This is particularly true of accidents involving human error and human factors in
general. Often, the basic causes assigned to an accident are, in fact, symptoms, in
contrast to root causes, a concept of considerable interest and importance. …
Technical deficiencies, when discovered, can be corrected rather easily, but most of
the repeat causes include human factors related deficiencies. These are more
difficult to correct, because these repeat causes are not usually root causes. For
example, one of the most common and serious repeat cause factors is flying in
adverse weather conditions; it is quite obvious that this is not a root cause. A root
cause has to answer the question “Why”? (Hopf-Weichel et al., 1979, p. 5-17)

“The aircraft lost oil pressure in all three of its engines in mid-flight. Two of the engines stopped, and the third gave out at about the time
the crew safely landed the aircraft ….
One of the tasks of mechanics is to replace an engine part, called a master chip detector, at scheduled intervals. The master chip
detector fits into the engine and is used to detect engine wear. O-rings are used to prevent oil leakage when the part is inserted. The two
mechanics for the flight in question had always gotten replacement master chip detectors from their foreman’s cabinet. These chip
detectors were all ready to go, with new O-rings installed. The mechanics’ work cards specified that new O-rings should be installed with
a space next to this instruction for their initials when the task was completed. However, their usual work situation meant that this step
was unnecessary, because someone else (apparently their supervisor) was already installing new O-rings on the chip detectors.
The night before the incident, an unusual event occurred. When the mechanics were ready to replace master chip detectors, they
found there were no chip detectors in the foreman’s cabinet. The mechanics had to get the parts from the stockroom. The chip detectors
were wrapped in a ‘semi-transparent sealed plastic package with a serviceable parts tag.’ The mechanics took the packages to the
aircraft and replaced the detectors in low light conditions. It turned out the chip detectors did not have O-rings attached. The mechanics
had not checked for them before installing them. There was a check procedure against improper seals: monitoring the engines to see if oil
leaked. The technicians did this, but apparently not for a long enough time to detect oil leaks. … They could not work strictly from
procedure; for example, the work card read ‘motor engine and check chip detector for leaks’ but it didn’t specify how long.”
